---
title: "Bayesian Statistics in Social Sciences"
subtitle: "Class 3 - Ssing *brms* for Simple Models"
author: "Wiktor Soral"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo=FALSE, 
  warning=FALSE, 
  message = FALSE,
  fig.align='center', 
  fig.dpi=300, 
  out.width='70%'
)
```

background-image: url("figures/football.webp")
background-position: 50% 80%
background-size: 60% 60%

## Summing random variables

- Suppose you and a thousand of your closest friends line up along the halfway line of a football field.

---

## Summing random variables


- Each of you has a coin in hand.

--

- At the sound of the whistle, you begin flipping your coins.

--

- Each time a coin lands heads, that person takes one step toward the left-hand goal.

--

- Each time a coin lands tails, that person takes one step toward the right-hand goal.

--

- Each person flips the coin 16 times, follows the resulting movements, and then stops.

--

- We then measure each person’s distance from the halfway line.


---
background-image: url("figures/football.webp")
background-position: 50% 80%
background-size: 60% 60%

## Summing random variables

- Can you predict what proportion of the thousand people will end up standing exactly on the halfway line?


---

## Summing random variables

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
theme_set(theme_classic())

# we set the seed to make the results of `runif()` reproducible.
set.seed(4)

pos <- 
  # make data with 100 people, 16 steps each with a starting point of `step == 0` (i.e., 17 rows per person)
  crossing(person = 1:100,
           step   = 0:16) %>% 
  # for all steps above `step == 0` simulate a `deviation`
  mutate(deviation = map_dbl(step, ~if_else(. == 0, 0, runif(1, -1, 1)))) %>% 
  # after grouping by `person`, compute the cumulative sum of the deviations, then `ungroup()`
  group_by(person) %>%
  mutate(position = cumsum(deviation)) %>% 
  ungroup() 

ggplot(data = pos, 
       aes(x = step, y = position, group = person)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(4, 8, 16), linetype = 2) +
  geom_line(aes(color = person < 2, alpha  = person < 2)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("skyblue4", "black")) +
  scale_alpha_manual(values = c(1/5, 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous("step number", breaks = c(0, 4, 8, 12, 16)) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```


---

## Summing random variables

```{r}
# Figure 4.2.a.
p1 <-
  pos %>%
  filter(step == 4) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = position)) +
  geom_line(stat = "density", color = "dodgerblue1") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-6, 6)) +
  labs(title = "4 steps")

# Figure 4.2.b.
p2 <-
  pos %>%
  filter(step == 8) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = position)) +
  geom_density(color = "dodgerblue2") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-6, 6)) +
  labs(title = "8 steps")

p3 <-
  pos %>%
  filter(step == 16) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = position)) +
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, 
                args = list(mean = 0, sd = 2.180408),
                linetype = 2) +  # 2.180408 came from the previous code block
  geom_density(color = "transparent", fill = "dodgerblue3", alpha = 1/2) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-6, 6)) +
  labs(title = "16 steps",
       y = "density")


library(patchwork)

# combine the ggplots
p1 | p2 | p3
```


---

## Why be Normal?

- Many phenomena arise as the sum of random variables. As a result, even if their distributions are not perfectly Gaussian, they tend to resemble a Normal distribution.

- When all we know—or choose to model—about a variable’s distribution is its mean and variance, the Normal distribution is the least informative and least surprising choice. 

- It is a natural expression of our uncertainty.

- See McElreath (2015)

---
class: inverse, center, middle

## Normal-Normal model

---

## Normal-Normal model

X is Normally distributed:

$$
X|\mu,\ \sigma_0^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma_0^2)
$$

$$
= (2\pi\sigma_0^2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}\mathrm{exp} \left[-\frac{1}{2\sigma_0^2}(X - \mu)^2\right]
$$

where 

$$
-\infty < \mu < \infty, \sigma_0^2\ \mathrm{known}
$$


---
## Normal-Normal model

The prior for $\mu$ is Normal:

$$
\mu|m,\ s^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(m, s^2)
$$


$$
= (2\pi s^2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}\mathrm{exp} \left[-\frac{1}{2s^2}(\mu - m)^2\right]
$$


$$
m,\ s^2 \mathrm{known}
$$


---

## Normal-Normal model

$$
\pi(\mu|\mathbf{x}) \propto p(\mathbf{x}|\mu)p(\mu)
$$

$$
\propto \prod_{i=1}^n\mathrm{exp} \left[-\frac{1}{2\sigma_0^2}(X - \mu)^2\right]\mathrm{exp} \left[-\frac{1}{2s^2}(\mu - m)^2\right]
$$

$$
\propto \left[-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{s^2} + \frac{n}{\sigma_0^2}\right)\left(\mu - \frac{\left(\frac{m}{s^2} + \frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma_0^2}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{s^2} + \frac{n}{\sigma_0^2}\right)}\right)\right]
$$

---

## Normal-Normal model

The posterior mean is:

$$
\hat{\mu} = \left. \left(\frac{m}{s^2} + \frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma_0^2}\right) \middle/ \left(\frac{1}{s^2} + \frac{n}{\sigma_0^2}\right) \right.
$$

and the posterior variance is:

$$
\hat{\sigma}_\mu^2 = \left(\frac{1}{s^2} + \frac{n}{\sigma_0^2}\right)^{-1}
$$

---
class: inverse, center, middle

## Switching to numerical approach

---

## Switching to numerical approach

- As model complexity increases, analytic solutions (i.e., closed-form formulas) often become impractical.
  - You may want to use priors or likelihoods other than the Normal distribution.
  - You may want to work with multivariate or multi-parameter models.


---

## Switching to numerical approach

- Therefore, we turn to numerical methods based on simulation. These methods allow us to approximate the posterior distribution without solving complex integrals.

- The most commonly used approach in Bayesian analysis is Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC). You will learn more about it in the next class.
